_sources.discrepancies: Ohio enacted HB 458 (2023) which changed the ID requirement from 'non-photo' to 'photo ID' effective 2024. NCSL categorizes Ohio as 'strict non-photo ID' in some versions — as of 2026 the correct characterization is photo ID required per the 2023 law change. Voters without acceptable photo ID may use the last four digits of their SSN plus address verification as an alternative (unique Ohio provision). TODO verify for 2026 cycle whether the HB 458 photo ID rules remain unchanged. Ohio primary is the first Tuesday after the first Monday in May. Ohio does offer early in-person voting starting 28 days before the election.

earlyVoting.notes: Early voting available at county board of elections offices starting 28 days before the election. TODO verify for 2026 cycle.
Early voting available at county board of elections starting 28 days before Election Day. Hours expand in final days before the election. TODO verify for 2026 cycle.
Early voting at county board of elections offices starts 28 days before the general election. Hours expand in the final week. TODO verify for 2026 cycle.

acceptedIds: Ohio driver's license or state ID card
Ohio bureau of motor vehicles photo ID
votingRules.expirationRule: Photo ID must not be expired. TODO verify for 2026 cycle — Ohio HB 458 (2023) rules apply.
votingRules.impedimentDeclaration: Voters without acceptable photo ID may provide the last four digits of their Social Security number and their address, and cast a regular ballot. Voters who cannot provide either may cast a provisional ballot.
votingRules.phonesAtPollsDetail: Ohio prohibits use of electronic devices, including phones, in a way that would capture images inside the voting booth or of another voter's ballot. Devices must be silenced in the polling place.
additionalRules: Semi-open primary: registered party members vote in their party's primary. Voters registered as 'independent' or with no party may choose either party's primary ballot.
No runoffs in Ohio — the candidate with the most votes wins.
Any registered voter may request an absentee (mail) ballot without a reason.
NOTE: Ohio photo ID law changed in 2023 (HB 458); previously non-photo ID was accepted. TODO verify current rules for 2026 cycle.

eligibility: Any registered voter may request an absentee ballot without providing a reason
voteByMail.applicationDeadline: 3 days before the election (received by the county board of elections)
voteByMail.returnDeadline: Must be received by the county board of elections by the time polls close on Election Day, or postmarked by Election Day and received within 10 days after
voteByMail.returnDeadlinePlain: Must arrive at the county board by poll closing on Election Day, or be postmarked by Election Day and received within 10 days after.
